message:
📌 *Dqn*
• DQN uses a neural net to estimate future rewards for actions, picking the one with the highest learned value.
• This works by learning from experience, but value function approximation and exploration are still hard; breaks easily.

📌 *Reranking Systems*
• Reranking refines initial retrieval by meticulously scoring a small candidate set for higher precision.
• It's critical when recall is easy but identifying the *best* items from many similar options is hard.

📌 *Mdp*
• MDPs model sequential decision-making; agent picks actions in states to maximize rewards, with outcomes dependent on current state.
• Critical for optimal control in dynamic systems; breaks down if state observations are incomplete (POMDP needed).

📌 *Isolation Forest*
• Outliers are identified by how few random splits it takes to isolate them in a tree structure.
• This method shines with high-dimensional data, but struggles with tightly clustered anomalies or global density variations.

📌 *Cvar*
• CVaR tells you the average hit you take when losses exceed your VaR threshold.
• It's critical for understanding "how bad" things get, especially when tails are fat.

📌 *Yield Curve*
• The yield curve plots a nation's government bond interest rates across maturities, typically rising with longer terms.
• Its shape reflects market expectations for interest rates and economic growth; an inverted curve often signals recession risk.

📌 *Eigenvalues Eigenvectors*
• Eigenvectors point in directions unchanged by a linear transformation; their eigenvalues are the scaling factors.
• Useful for simplifying complex matrix operations, but works only for square matrices that are diagonalizable.

📌 *Rmse Metric*
• RMSE is your average prediction error, but big mistakes hurt disproportionately, measured in the same units as what you're forecasting.
• Use RMSE for regression where large errors are costly; if RMSE is much higher than MAE, you likely have problematic outliers.

📌 *Drawdown*
• Drawdown is your portfolio's percentage drop from its highest prior value.
• Max Drawdown (MDD) quantifies worst-case loss; Calmar ratio uses it to gauge risk-adjusted returns.

📌 *Perplexity Eval*
• Perplexity quantifies a language model's uncertainty – lower means it's less 'surprised' by the text.
• It's an effective branching factor; high perplexity indicates the model is struggling to predict.
